sections.heading: Part I (Prima Pars): God and Creation
sections.html:
Sacra Doctrina, the Five Ways, and the Divine Nature

Aquinas opens the Summa with a defense of sacred theology as a genuine science (scientia) in the Aristotelian sense — a body of knowledge derived from first principles, even though in theology those principles are the articles of faith received on divine authority rather than self-evident truths apprehended by unaided reason. Theology's proper object is God himself — not merely as the conclusion of philosophical argument but as he has freely revealed himself. Aquinas's method throughout is to proceed by quaestio: posing a question, marshaling objections, giving a definitive response (respondeo), and replying to each objection in turn. This dialectical structure reflects his conviction that faith and reason, properly ordered, do not conflict but cooperate — grace does not destroy nature but perfects it.

The existence of God is demonstrated in Question 2, Article 3 through the celebrated Five Ways (Quinque Viae). These are not meant as proofs in the modern deductive sense but as philosophical demonstrations following the evidence of the created order back to a First Cause. The first way argues from motion: whatever is moved is moved by another, and there cannot be an infinite regress of movers, therefore there is a First Unmoved Mover. The second argues from efficient causation: nothing is its own cause; a regress of causes requires a First Uncaused Cause. The third argues from contingency: contingent beings might not have existed; if everything were contingent there would have been nothing; therefore there is a necessary being. The fourth argues from degrees of perfection: things are more or less good, true, and noble by reference to a maximum, which must itself exist. The fifth argues from teleological order: unintelligent things act toward ends as though directed by a mind, which implies an intelligent director.

Having established God's existence, Aquinas proceeds to describe the divine nature through the via negativa (what God is not) and via analogiae (what can be said by analogy from creatures). Divine simplicity (God has no parts — no matter, no form distinct from act, no essence distinct from existence) is the foundation: in God, essence and existence are identical, which is why God alone can say I AM WHO I AM:

Exodus 3:14 — "God said to Moses, 'I AM WHO I AM.'"
From simplicity follows perfection, goodness, infinity, immutability, and eternity. God is ipsum esse subsistens — subsistent being itself — and all creaturely perfections exist in him in an eminent and undivided way. Language about God is analogical: when we say God is good or wise, we speak neither univocally (exactly as creatures are good) nor equivocally (in a completely unrelated sense) but analogically — the perfections in creatures are reflections, at a creaturely remove, of perfections that exist in God perfectly and in himself.

The Trinity: Processions, Relations, and Persons

Aquinas's Trinitarian theology (Questions 27–43) is among the most architecturally precise in the Western tradition. He begins not with the persons but with the processions — the immanent acts within the Godhead from which the personal distinctions arise. God is pure act, and his knowing and loving are not accidental but essential activities. The Father's act of self-knowledge generates the Word — an inner locution that is the Son:

John 1:1 — "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God."
The Father's and Son's mutual love produces a single spiration, which is the Holy Spirit. These two processions — generation (the Word from the intellect) and spiration (Love from the will) — are the immanent foundations of the economic Trinity's engagement in creation and salvation.

From the processions Aquinas derives the relations: paternity, filiation, active spiration, and passive spiration. The divine persons are constituted by these relations — they are, technically, subsistent relations. The Father is not a substance that has the relation of paternity; the Father just is the relation of paternity subsisting in the divine essence. This precision serves the purpose of affirming real distinction between the persons (lest Sabellianism be affirmed) while maintaining absolute divine simplicity (lest tritheism be affirmed). The persons are really distinct from one another but really identical with the divine essence.

The missions of the Son and Spirit in salvation history — the Incarnation and Pentecost — are the temporal extensions of the eternal processions. The Son is sent by the Father in the fullness of time (Galatians 4:4); the Spirit is sent by the Father and the Son on the day of Pentecost (John 15:26). These missions are not impositions from outside but the temporal manifestation of what the divine persons eternally are to one another. Aquinas grounds the entire economy of salvation in the Trinitarian life: salvation is the creature's participation, through grace, in the very life that Father, Son, and Spirit share eternally.

Creation, the Angels, and the Image of God in the Human Person

Aquinas treats creation ex nihilo as a truth knowable by reason (since only God is self-existent; everything else must have received its being from another) and confirmed by revelation. Creation is a free act of divine love — God did not create out of need or necessity but to communicate his goodness to creatures. The universe is not an emanation from God's substance (Neoplatonism) nor a formation of pre-existing matter (Platonism) but a genuine bringing-into-being of what was simply nothing. This establishes the absolute ontological distinction between Creator and creation — a distinction that makes both science and theology possible.

Aquinas devotes considerable attention to the angels (Questions 50–64), whose existence and nature Scripture attests and whose philosophical analysis illuminates the nature of immaterial being. Angels are pure forms — they have no matter, and therefore each angel is its own species, an individual that exhausts a kind. They possess intellect and will without bodily organs; their knowledge is not discursive (moving from premise to conclusion) but intuitive, received in a single act at creation. The fall of the angels — Lucifer's refusal of subjection to God — illustrates the nature of sin as the creature's turning toward its own excellence in defiance of its ordained end.

The human person is Aquinas's most complex creature: a composite of matter and form, body and soul, sharing intellectual nature with the angels but embodied nature with the animals. The soul is the form of the body, not a ghost in a machine — body and soul together constitute the single human substance. The imago Dei resides primarily in the intellect and will: the human person images God through the capacity for self-reflective knowledge (an analogy to the Word) and for free, rational love (an analogy to the Spirit).

Genesis 1:26–27 — "Then God said, 'Let us make man in our image, after our likeness'... So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him."
Original justice — the preternatural condition of Adam before the Fall — involved a supernatural gift (donum superadditum) that held the lower faculties in subjection to reason and reason in subjection to God. The loss of this gift at the Fall disrupted these right orderings, leaving human nature weakened, confused, and inclined toward disorder.

  • Exodus 3:14
  • John 1:1
  • Galatians 4:4
  • John 15:26
  • Genesis 1:26–27

sections.heading: Part I–II (Prima Secundae): The Last End and Human Acts
sections.html:
The Last End: Beatitude as the Vision of God

The Prima Secundae opens with a question that governs all of moral theology: what is the ultimate end of human life? Aquinas follows Aristotle in organizing ethics around the concept of eudaimonia — flourishing or happiness — but he decisively transforms the Aristotelian analysis. For Aristotle, the highest human good was the philosophical contemplation of truth, achievable in principle by the excellent use of natural human capacities. For Aquinas, this falls short: no finite good — not wealth, honor, pleasure, power, or even philosophical wisdom — can satisfy the human will, which is ordered to the Good itself. The intellect's capacity for truth and the will's capacity for goodness are infinite in their scope; only God, who is infinite Good and infinite Truth, can fill them.

Beatitude, therefore, is not merely a heightened form of natural happiness but a supernatural gift: the direct vision of God's essence (visio beatifica), in which the human intellect is elevated by the light of glory (lumen gloriae) to see God as he sees himself. This is not a merely poetic aspiration but a precise metaphysical claim: the intellect, by its created nature, cannot attain this vision through its own power; it requires a created quality infused by God that elevates it beyond its natural capacity. Aquinas grounds this in Paul's language of eschatological transformation:

1 Corinthians 13:12 — "For now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known."
The beatific vision is the fulfillment of both the Aristotelian teleology (creatures reaching their end) and the Augustinian desire (the restless heart finding rest in God). Grace does not cancel nature; it completes what nature begins but cannot finish.

Human Acts, the Passions, and the Cardinal Virtues

Aquinas's analysis of human action is systematic and fine-grained. A human act (as opposed to an act of a human, like digestion) is one that proceeds from deliberate reason and free will. Aquinas maps the structure of voluntary action through a series of paired acts of intellect and will: apprehension and wish (simplex volitio), judgment of the good and intention, deliberation and consent, practical judgment (electio) and choice, command and use. This architecture serves the purpose of locating exactly where freedom and responsibility reside — and therefore where virtue and vice are formed.

The passions of the soul — joy, sadness, hope, fear, love, hatred, desire, aversion, anger, and the rest — are not morally suspect in themselves. Aquinas rehabilitates the passions against any Stoic indifference: properly ordered affections are part of moral excellence, not obstacles to it. A person who does the right thing but feels no appropriate emotion is not more virtuous but less. The goal of moral formation is the right ordering of reason, will, and affection together.

The four cardinal virtues — prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance — are the fundamental habits that constitute a well-ordered human character. Prudence (prudentia) is the master virtue: the intellectual habit of discerning the right means to the good end in any concrete situation. Without prudence, the other virtues cannot function properly, because they all require practical wisdom about how to act in particular circumstances. Justice orders the will in relation to others. Fortitude moderates the irascible appetites in the face of danger and hardship. Temperance moderates the concupiscible appetites. Each virtue has corresponding vices at both extremes. Aquinas grounds the unity of virtue in prudence: one who lacks prudence does not truly possess any of the moral virtues, because the moral virtues require prudent direction:

Proverbs 8:14 — "I have counsel and sound wisdom; I have insight; I have strength."

Law and Grace: Eternal, Natural, Human, and Divine Law

Aquinas's treatise on law is among the most influential texts in Western legal and political thought. He distinguishes four kinds of law in a hierarchical order. The eternal law (lex aeterna) is the rational governance of the universe by God — the divine wisdom as directive of all creaturely acts and movements toward their ends. The natural law (lex naturalis) is the rational creature's participation in the eternal law: the innate tendency of human reason to grasp the first principles of practical life — do good, avoid evil, preserve life, live in society, seek truth. Natural law is not a separate system alongside revelation but the created infrastructure of moral knowledge that grace presupposes and builds upon.

Human law is the application of natural law to particular social circumstances — the positive law of states, which derives its authority from natural law and, ultimately, from the eternal law. Human laws that contradict the natural law are not true laws but corruptions of law, and do not bind in conscience. The divine law — Scripture, both Old and New Testament — supplements natural law, providing explicit guidance where reason is uncertain, correcting the distortions introduced by sin, and directing human beings toward the supernatural end that natural reason cannot know.

The Old Law Aquinas treats as genuinely good and divinely given, but as pedagogical and provisional — its ceremonial and judicial precepts pointed forward to Christ and were abrogated by his coming. The moral precepts of the Old Law (summarized in the Decalogue) remain in force because they articulate the natural law. The New Law is primarily the grace of the Holy Spirit — it is the interior law written on the heart, fulfilling what the external law commanded:

Jeremiah 31:33 — "I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts."
2 Corinthians 3:3 — "You are a letter from Christ delivered by us, written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts."
Grace includes the infused virtues — faith, hope, and charity, along with the infused moral virtues — that elevate the soul's operations to the supernatural order and enable it to act in ways oriented toward the beatific vision. Justification, for Aquinas, involves the infusion of grace, the movement of free will toward God, the remission of sin, and the movement of free will away from sin — a structured, Spirit-wrought transformation of the whole person.

  • 1 Corinthians 13:12
  • Proverbs 8:14
  • Jeremiah 31:33
  • 2 Corinthians 3:3
  • Romans 2:14–15

sections.heading: Part II–II (Secunda Secundae): The Virtues in Detail
sections.html:
Faith, Hope, and Charity — the Theological Virtues

The Secunda Secundae is Aquinas's most expansive section, moving through the theological and cardinal virtues with their associated acts, gifts, and opposing vices. The theological virtues — faith, hope, and charity — are distinguished from the cardinal virtues by their object: they have God himself as their immediate object, rather than proximate human goods. They are also infused rather than acquired: they are given by God in the act of justification and cannot be developed by natural moral training.

Faith (fides) Aquinas defines as the habit by which the intellect is moved to assent to what is not seen, under the motion of the will as moved by grace. Its formal object is the First Truth — God as he has revealed himself — and its material object is everything God has revealed. Aquinas's famous definition draws on Paul's:

Hebrews 11:1 — "Now faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen."
Faith is not mere historical belief (the demons believe and tremble) nor is it intellectual certainty of the beatific-vision kind; it occupies a middle position — it is more certain than opinion, in that it is held without doubt by the motion of grace, but it does not enjoy the direct evidence that vision would provide. Unformed faith (fides informis) is distinguished from formed faith (fides formata): the latter is faith animated by charity, which alone is saving.

Charity (caritas) is the form of all the virtues — the virtue that directs all other virtuous acts toward the ultimate end. Without charity, the other virtues, though genuine in their own order, do not contribute to eternal life. Paul's celebrated passage remains the locus classicus:

1 Corinthians 13:13 — "So now faith, hope, and love abide, these three; but the greatest of these is love."
Charity's primacy lies in its object — it tends directly toward God himself, not merely toward goods associated with God. The seven gifts of the Holy Spirit (wisdom, understanding, counsel, fortitude, knowledge, piety, fear of the Lord) are dispositions infused by the Spirit that perfect the virtues, making the soul docile and responsive to the Spirit's movements in a way that goes beyond what the acquired or even infused virtues achieve by their own operation.

Prudence, Justice, and the Virtue of Religion

Aquinas's treatment of prudence and justice in the Secunda Secundae expands the brief treatment in the Prima Secundae into a comprehensive analysis of their parts, species, and opposing vices. Prudence's integral parts include memory (learning from the past), understanding of principles, docility (teachableness), shrewdness, reason, foresight, circumspection, and caution. The opposing vices — imprudence, negligence, and the various forms of worldly cunning that mimic prudence — receive precise diagnosis. Conscience, for Aquinas, is an act of prudence: the application of moral knowledge to a particular case. An erroneous conscience binds, even when it errs, but the person with a culpably erroneous conscience is still responsible for the error.

Justice is the virtue that orders the will to render to each person what is due. Aquinas distinguishes general justice (ordered to the common good) from particular justice (ordered to individuals). Within particular justice, commutative justice governs exchanges between individuals and requires restitution when another's goods or rights have been violated. Distributive justice governs the community's allocation of common goods and burdens. The demands of restitution are worked out in elaborate detail — Aquinas's analysis of property, debt, fraud, and theft became foundational for later Catholic social thought.

Under the virtue of justice falls the virtue of religion (religio) — the habit of rendering to God the worship that is his due. Aquinas distinguishes latria (worship owed to God alone) from dulia (honor owed to the saints) and hyperdulia (the higher honor owed to Mary). This distinction was crucial in the medieval controversy over image veneration and remains central to Catholic liturgical theology. Prayer, devotion, vows, and sacrifice are all acts of the virtue of religion. The common good occupies a structurally important position throughout: Aquinas consistently subordinates individual goods to the common good in ways that resist both collectivism (where the individual is merely an instrument of the state) and extreme individualism (where social bonds are merely contractual):

Romans 13:7 — "Pay to all what is owed to them: taxes to whom taxes are owed, revenue to whom revenue is owed, respect to whom respect is owed, honor to whom honor is owed."

The Contemplative Life, Prophecy, and the States of Perfection

The Secunda Secundae closes with a series of questions on charisms and states of life that reflect the intellectual and institutional world of thirteenth-century Christendom. Aquinas distinguishes the active life (ordered to external works of service and justice) from the contemplative life (ordered to the direct consideration of truth, above all the truth of God). Both are legitimate Christian vocations; neither is simply superior. But Aquinas follows Gregory the Great and the monastic tradition in according contemplation a certain priority: it is directed immediately to the love of God and corresponds more closely to the activity of the beatific vision that is our ultimate end. Martha's busyness is necessary and good; Mary's sitting at the Lord's feet is the better part:

Luke 10:41–42 — "Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things, but one thing is necessary. Mary has chosen the good portion, which will not be taken away from her."

Prophecy Aquinas treats as a gratuitous charism — a gift given not primarily for the prophet's own sanctification but for the benefit of others and the building up of the church. It involves a supernatural illumination of the intellect by which the prophet knows what could not be known by natural powers. Prophecy does not require holiness in the prophet (Balaam prophesied; Caiaphas prophesied unwittingly), though the perfection of prophecy belongs to those who also possess charity. The gift ceased in its classical biblical form with the close of the apostolic age, though Aquinas acknowledges ongoing forms of divine illumination in the life of the church.

The states of perfection — the religious life and the episcopate — are treated at the end of the Secunda Secundae. Religious life (the vowed life of poverty, chastity, and obedience in a monastic or mendicant order) is a state of perfection not because those in it are already perfect but because they have committed themselves to a comprehensive set of means for the pursuit of charity. The three evangelical counsels (poverty, chastity, obedience) are not commands binding on all Christians but voluntary embracings of goods that remove particular obstacles to perfect charity. Bishops occupy a state of perfection that is, in a sense, more perfect still — they are not merely pursuing perfection for themselves but are responsible for communicating it to others, in an analogy to Christ the Good Shepherd who laid down his life for the sheep.

  • Hebrews 11:1
  • 1 Corinthians 13:13
  • Romans 13:7
  • Luke 10:41–42

sections.heading: Part III (Tertia Pars): Christ and the Sacraments
sections.html:
The Incarnation: Fittingness, the Hypostatic Union, and the Satisfaction of Christ

The Tertia Pars is the crown of the Summa, moving from God and moral theology to the person and work of Jesus Christ. Aquinas opens with the question of fittingness (convenientia): was it fitting for God to become man? He argues that it was supremely fitting on multiple grounds. It manifests the greatest possible communication of divine goodness to creatures. It provides the most efficacious remedy for sin (one whose dignity as a divine person makes his satisfaction infinite). It furnishes the supreme example of virtue. And it reveals the depth of God's love:

John 3:16 — "For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life."
Fittingness does not mean necessity — God could have saved humanity by other means had he willed to — but it means that the Incarnation is not an arbitrary response to sin but the most perfectly appropriate one.

The hypostatic union is Aquinas's central Christological concern. Christ is one person (the eternal Son, the second Person of the Trinity) in two natures — the divine nature and the human nature — united without confusion, mixture, division, or separation, in the formula of Chalcedon. Aquinas works through the implications of this with great precision. Christ's human soul possessed the beatific vision from the moment of conception (since it was united to the divine person). Christ possessed infused knowledge of all things his mission required. His human will was sinless but genuinely free. The suffering and death of Christ were real and voluntary sufferings of the human nature, but their merit was infinite because of the dignity of the divine person who bore them.

The Passion of Christ is the efficient, meritorious, and exemplary cause of salvation. Aquinas works out the theory of satisfaction with care: Adam's sin was an offence of infinite gravity because God is the one offended, but only finite satisfaction was available from human beings. The God-man alone can make infinite satisfaction because his dignity as a divine person renders his acts of infinite meritorious value. Christ's Passion was thus more than sufficient — superabundantly sufficient — to atone for all human sin:

Romans 5:10 — "For if while we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son, much more, now that we are reconciled, shall we be saved by his life."
The cross is not merely a demonstration of love but a real transaction in which the demands of justice are met by one who stands in our place.

Christ's Mysteries and the Resurrection

Following the general treatment of Christ's person and saving work, Aquinas gives detailed attention to the individual mysteries of Christ's life. The baptism of Christ by John is treated not as a cleansing for Christ (who had no sin) but as a consecration of the sacrament of baptism and a manifestation of the Trinity: the Father speaks, the Spirit descends as a dove, the Son is baptized. The Transfiguration is interpreted as a proleptic revelation of the glory that will characterize Christ's risen and glorified body — a foretaste of the beatitude that is the destiny of his disciples:

Matthew 17:2 — "And he was transfigured before them, and his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became white as light."

The Resurrection occupies a central position in the Tertia Pars. Christ's Resurrection is the cause — not merely the pattern or promise — of our resurrection. Aquinas distinguishes the efficient causality of the Resurrection: by raising Christ, God implanted in Christ's glorified body the power that will, at the final resurrection, communicate resurrection life to all the dead. The risen Christ is not merely a resuscitated corpse but the firstborn from the dead, whose glorified body participates fully in the properties of glory — impassibility, agility, subtlety, and clarity:

1 Corinthians 15:20 — "But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep."
The Ascension is the crowning of Christ's exaltation: he enters heaven with his glorified human nature, opening the way for his people, and sits at the right hand of the Father in his role as eternal High Priest and King:
Ephesians 1:20 — "He raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly places."

The Sacraments: Definition, Cause, and the Eucharist

The final section of the Tertia Pars treats the sacraments of the New Law — the seven rites instituted by Christ as causes and signs of grace. A sacrament, for Aquinas, is a sign of a sacred thing insofar as it sanctifies human beings. The seven sacraments — Baptism, Confirmation, Eucharist, Penance, Anointing, Orders, and Matrimony — correspond to the stages and needs of the natural human life, elevated to the supernatural order: birth (Baptism), growth (Confirmation), nourishment (Eucharist), healing (Penance and Anointing), social structure (Orders and Matrimony). The sacraments cause grace instrumentally — as instruments of Christ's power they contain and confer the grace they signify, ex opere operato (by the very performance of the rite, given proper disposition in the recipient).

Baptism receives extended analysis: it imprints an indelible spiritual character on the soul, signifying the person's permanent configuration to Christ; it remits both original sin and all actual sin together with all punishment due to sin; it is necessary for salvation in the ordinary order (though Aquinas acknowledges baptism of desire and baptism of blood). The character of Baptism means that it cannot be repeated — a principle grounded in the permanence of the spiritual reality it creates.

The Eucharist is the sacrament to which the rest are ordered — the consummation and center of the sacramental life. Aquinas develops the doctrine of transubstantiation with his characteristic philosophical precision: the substance of bread and wine is converted into the body and blood of Christ (transubstantiatio), while the accidents (color, taste, weight) remain without a subject, sustained miraculously by divine power. Christ is wholly present under each species, and under each part of each species — the totus Christus is received even when the host is broken:

John 6:53–55 — "So Jesus said to them, 'Truly, truly, I say to you, unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you... For my flesh is true food, and my blood is true drink.'"
The Summa breaks off unfinished — Aquinas ceased writing in December 1273, reportedly saying that all he had written seemed like straw compared to what he had seen. The Supplement, compiled posthumously from his earlier Commentary on the Sentences, completes the treatment of Penance, Anointing, Orders, Matrimony, and the Last Things. The unfinished nature of the work is itself a testimony to the inexhaustibility of its subject.

  • John 3:16
  • Romans 5:10
  • Matthew 17:2
  • 1 Corinthians 15:20
  • Ephesians 1:20
  • John 6:53–55
